Facts.text: The First Crusaders begin the siege of Hosn al-Akrad, Syria
Flemish Dutch coast ravaged by north westerly storm
By Edict of Orleans French persecution of Huguenots is suspended
Articles of the Warsaw Confederation are signed, granting religious freedom in Poland
Sir Thomas Warner founds the first English colony in the Caribbean, on Saint Kitts.
English parliament resolves that the throne is vacant
British writer Horace Walpole, in a letter to Horace Mann, coins the word serendipity
Pownal, Vermont created by Benning Wentworth as one of the New Hampshire Grants
Philadelphia's Free Africa Society organizes
Lord Gordon found guilty of libel of Queen of France
London's Pall Mall becomes the first street lit by gaslight
French writer Stendhal's first book is published
William Kneass is appointed as the 3rd US Chief Engraver (1824-40)
Opera "Fra Diavolo" by Daniel Auber, premieres in Paris
Battle of Allwal, British beat Sikhs in Punjab
King of Naples grants his subjects a constitution
Northwestern University is chartered in Chicago
The first locomotive runs from the Atlantic Ocean to the Pacific Ocean on the Panama Railway
Britain formally returns the Mosquito Coast to Nicaragua
Battle of New Bern, North Carolina
North German Confederation, led by Prussian King Wilhelm I, captures Paris after the city surrenders, ending a four-month siege
George Coy, Herrick Frost, and Walter Lewis open first commercial telephone exchange, in New Haven, Connecticut
Yale Daily News is published, the first college daily newspaper
Battle at Laing's Neck Natal: Boers beat superior powered British
During a snowstorm at Fort Keogh, Montana, the world's largest snowflakes are reported to be 15 inches (38 cm) wide and 8 inches (20 cm) thick
England is all out for 45 against Australia at the SCG, its lowest total ever
Edward Mcdowell's "Hamlet & Ophelia" premieres in Boston
American Social Science Association incorporated by Congress
Carnegie Institute founded in Washington, D.C.
1st college sports letters given to Seniors who played on University of Chicago's football team are awarded blankets with letter "C" on them
US military forces leave Cuba for 2nd time
Frenchman Henri Rougier wins 1st Rally of Monte Carlo
Beverly Hills, California, is incorporated
1st US ship lost in WW I, William P. Frye (carrying wheat to UK)
The United States Coast Guard is created by merging the US Life Saving Service & the US Revenue Cutter Service
German colony of Cameroon surrenders to Britain & France
Manitoba women are the first in Canada to win the right to vote and to hold provincial office
Opera "Goyescas" premieres in New York City
US PresidentWoodrow Wilsonnominates Louis Brandeis for the Supreme Court of the United States
Municipally owned streetcars take to the streets of San Francisco, California
Finnish Civil War: Rebels seized control of the capital, Helsinki, and members of the Senate of Finland go underground
Strike on Berlin ammunition factory
J E Clair turns Green Bay franchise back to NFL
Knickerbocker Storm, Washington, D.C.'s biggest snowfall, causes the city's greatest loss of life when the roof of the Knickerbocker Theater collapses, killing 98 patrons
Demonstration against a Dutch University in Ghent
-46°F (-43°C), Pittsburgh, New Hampshire (state record)
Serbian-Croatian-Slavic government of Oezonowitsj falls
Christopher Hornsrud chosen Prime Minister of Norway
1st US state unemployment insurance act enacted (Wisconsin)
Japanese forces attack Shanghai.
French government of Paul Boncour falls
German government of Von Schleicher falls
The name "Pakistan" is coined by Choudhry Rahmat Ali and gradually accepted by Muslims in the Indian subcontinent, who use it to push for a separate Muslim homeland in South Asia
1st US ski tow (rope) begins operation (Woodstock, Vermont)
Iceland becomes the first Western country to legalize abortion
Chicago's Les Cunningham scores 5 points in one period to set an NHL record that stands until 1978; records 2 goals & 3 assists in a 10:04 span during the 3d period of an 8-1 win over Montreal
French-Thai War: Final air battle of the conflict. Japanese-mediated armistice goes into effect later in the day.
Soviet troops under General Semyon Timoshenko's troops move into Ukraine
WW II Navy flier Don Mason sends message "Sighted sub sank same"
Chic Blackhawks beats NY Rangers 10-1, Max Bentley scores 4 goals
WWII: 683 British RAF bombers attack Berlin, Germany
WWII: German submarines U-271 is sunk by US Navy air patrol and U-571 by Australian plane off the coast of Ireland
Dutch airplanes dump pamphlets on Java
General "Vinegar Joe" Stillwell and truck convoy reopen Burma Road to China
Swedish ships bring food to starving Netherlands
"Bay Psalm" book auctioned for a record $151,000
A plane crash in the Diablo mountains kills 28 bracero farm workers being sent back to Mexico, inspiring Woodie Guthrie's song "Deportee"
David Diamond's 4th Symphony, dedicated to Natalie Koussevitzky, premieres with the Boston Symphony Orchestra conducted byLeonard Bernstein
NY Giants sign their 1st black players, Monte Irvin & Ford Smith
UN Security council convicts Dutch aggression in Indonesia
Preston Tucker, auto maker, found not guilty of mail fraud
"La Vie Commence Demain," the first X-rated movie depicting artificial insemination, opens in London

American and Bermudan naturalist discover nesting site of Bermuda petrel, a sea bird also known as cahow; the species was thought extinct since 1615, but sporadic sightings spawned an expedition to some remote rocky crags off of Bermuda
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
19-year old Derek Bentley is hanged in Wandsworth Prison, London, controversially convicted of the murder of a police officer. He was pardoned on 30th July 1998.
WJTV TV channel 12 in Jackson, MS (CBS) begins broadcasting
1954 NFL Draft: Bobby Garrett from University of Stanford first pick by Cleveland Browns
Austrian skier Toni Sailer wins the giant slalom at the Cortina d'Ampezzo Winter Olympics on his way to a sweep of the 3 alpine skiing events at the Games
Italy goes 1-2 in the 2-man bobsleigh at the Cortina d'Ampezzo Winter Olympics; along with silver in 4-man event, the only medals for the home nation at the Games
Construction begins on 1st private thorium-uranium nuclear reactor
The Lego Group patents their design of interlocking Lego bricks, still compatible with bricks produced today
Soviet Union wins 62-37 for 1st international basketball loss by US
1st photograph bounced off Moon, Washington, D.C.
NFL announces Dallas Cowboys (1960) & Minnesota Vikings (1961) franchises
Republic of Rwanda proclaimed
Johanne Relleke gets stung by bees 2,443 times in Rhodesia & survives
"Still" single released by Bill Anderson (Billboard No. 1 U.S. Hot Country Singles, 1963)
-34°F (-37°C), Cynthiana, Kentucky (state record)
"The Who" make their 1st appearance on British television programme "Ready Steady Go!"
29th PGA Seniors Golf Championship: Chandler Harper
Goose Goslin & Kiki Cuyler elected to Baseball Hall of Fame
Radiation alert following B-52 crash in Arctic air base of Thule in Greenland
Thai boxer Chartchai Chionoi retains his world flyweight title with a 13th-round TKO of home favourite Efren Torres in Mexico City; first of 3 meetings between the pair
Lubomír Strougal succeeds Cernik as premier of Czechoslovakia
1971 NFL Draft: Jim Plunkett from Stanford University first pick by New England Patriots
Oral Roberts' Eddie Woods grabs 30 rebounds for 2nd consecutive game
The Northern Ireland Civil Rights Association place "special emphasis on the necessity for a peaceful incident-free day" at the next march on 30 January in an effort to avoid violence
"Barnaby Jones" premieres on CBS TV
In the run up to the first anniversary of 'Bloody Sunday' there is serious rioting in Derry, North Ireland
Mickey Welch, George Kelly & Billy Evans elected to Baseball Hall of Fame
NHL Detroit Red Wings Henry Boucha scores 6 seconds into game in 4-2 win over Canadiens at The Forum in Montreal, Quebec
Ron Howard appears on M*A*S*H in "Sometimes You Hear the Bullet"
1975 NFL Draft: Steve Bartkowski from University of California first pick by Atlanta Falcons
E. A. S. Prasanna takes 8-76 to rip through NZ at Eden Park
NBA Atlanta Hawks begin a 28 game road losing streak
Ranger's Don Murdoch failed on 4th penalty shot against Islanders
Ted Nugent autographs a fan's arm with his knife
New York coach Red Holzman wins his 600th NBA game, only the second (after Red Auerbach) coach at the time to reach the plateau, as the Knicks beat Golden State, 107-103
USCGC Blackthorn (WLB-391) collides with the tanker Capricorn while leaving Tampa Florida and capsizes killing 23 Coast Guard crewmembers.
Olympic Glory tanker at Galveston Bay, Texas, spills 1 million gallons of oil in a ship collision
Italian police rescue US Brigadier General James Dozier, held hostage for six weeks by the Red Brigade of Padua, Italy
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Record 295,000 dominoes toppled in Fuerth, West Germany
Houston'sBill Fitchbecomes the 6th coach in NBA history to post 600 wins as the Rockets beat the New Jersey Nets, 97-93
Angolan revolutionary and leader of UNITA, Jonas Savimbi visits Washington, D.C.
Wrestler Jim Neidhart indicted for assaulting a flight attendant
Canada's Supreme court declares anti-abortion law unconstitutional
Denver Nuggets point guard Michael Adams takes record streak to 79 consecutive games with at least one 3-point field goal in 117-102 loss against Indiana at Market Square Arena; streak ends next game
"Independent on Sunday" begins publishing in London
Romanians protest against Ion Iliescu government
"A Closer Look" with Faith Daniels premieres on NBC-TV
Dictator Siad Barre flees Somalia ending 22 year rule
Helicopter crashes into office building in San Jose California, 1 dead
Inna Lassovskaja jumps ladies world record (14.78m)
Memphis Mad Dogs granted CFL's 13th franchise
Super Bowl XXX, Sun Devil Stadium, Tempe, AZ: Dallas Cowboys beat Pittsburgh Steelers, 27-17; MVP: Larry Brown, Dallas, CB
TAME Flight 120, a Boeing 727-100 crashes in the Andes mountains in southern Colombia killing 92
Death toll from coordinated bombing attacks in Kano, Nigeria, reaches 185
10 people are killed and 11 are injured in after a freight train collides with a bus in Heihe City, China
20 people are killed after a Bombardier CRJ200 airliner crashes outside Almaty, Kazakhstan
Iran’s Pishgam rocket successfully completes a return trip of sending a monkey into space
DNA analysis confirms that the 6th century Plague of Justinian was caused by a variant of Yersinia pestis (the same bacteria for the Black Death)
Peter Cosgrove is named the next Governor-General of Australia
Seventh Republican presidential candidates debate hosted by Fox, held in Des Moines, Iowa
French drivers Sebastien Ogier and Julien Ingrassia win their 6th Monte Carlo Rally in an M-Sport Ford WRT
Seven survivors from missing Kiribati ferry carrying 100 people rescued after week at sea
Draft framework for peace agreement to end 17-year conflict in Afghanistan agreed by US and Afghan negotiators
US Justice Department charges Chinese firm Huawei with bank and wire fraud
Mexico's COVID-19 death toll becomes the world's third highest, overtaking India's at 155,145
Share trading app Robinhood, favored by Reddit, suspends trading of Gamestop amid huge surge in share price of the previously short listed stock by Wall Street
100 Ballads names "The Wandering Prince of Troy" the most popular ballad of the 17th century in a project documenting "the earliest form of commercial pop music in England"
Drone attack on US military base in Jordan kills three American soldiers and injures 40
England cricket spin bowler Tom Hartley follows Ollie Pope's 196 with stunning debut figures of 7/62 as the tourists upset India by 28 runs in the 1st Test in Hyderabad
Environmental protesters throw soup at Leonardo da Vinci's "Mona Lisa" - protected by glass at the Louvre in Paris
The West Indies win their first cricket Test match in Australia in 27 years in Brisbane; rookie fast bowler Shamar Joseph takes 7/68 to guide Windies to an 8 run victory and a 1-1 series tie
German KingHenry IVis absolved by Holy Roman Emperor Pope Gregory VII and invited back to the Church after his penitent Walk to Canossa
Bal des Ardents - fire during a royal court ball in Paris kills four dancers dressed as wild men, with KingCharles VInarrowly escaping
Pope Alexander VI (Roderic Llançol i de Borgia) gives his son Cesare as hostage toCharles VIIIof France
Holy Roman EmperorCharles Vopens the Diet of Worms in Worms, Germany, which lasts until May 25 and produces the "Edict of Worms" denouncingMartin Luther
9-year-oldEdward VIsucceedsHenry VIIIas King of England
Scottish KingJames VIsigns 2nd Confession of Faith (Kings Confession), promising to adhere to the protestant faith
Welsh pirateHenry Morgancaptures Panama City from its Spanish defenders
The Russian Academy of Sciences founded in St. Petersburg byPeter the Great, and implemented in a Senate decree. Called St. Petersburg Academy of Sciences until 1917.
Frederick Northbecomes Prime Minister of Great Britain afterAugustus FitzRoyresigns
Jane Austen's "Pride and Prejudice" is published by Thomas Egerton in the United Kingdom
British colonial officerStamford Raffleslands in Singapore
Confederate PresidentJefferson Davisnames his three commissioners to negotiate for peace with the Union
Commission forAuguste Rodin's sculpture 'The Burghers of Calais' signed by the Municipal Council of Calais.
US PresidentWoodrow Wilsonrefuses to prohibit immigration of illiterates
Spanish dictatorMiguel Primo de Riveraresigns as Prime Minister of Spain after losing the support of his fellow military leaders and KingAlfonso XIII
Pravda criticizesDmitri Shostakovich's opera "Lady Macbeth of the Mtsensk District"
Revival of George Gershwin, DuBose Heyward, andIra Gershwin's folk-opera "Porgy & Bess" featuring Anne Brown and Todd Duncan, opens on The Majestic Theatre, NYC,
Leonard Bernstein's Symphony No. 1 "Jeremiah" premieres, with the composer conducting the Pittsburgh Symphony Orchestra at the Syria Mosque in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania
PresidentManuel Roxasgranted full amnesty to all so-called Philippine collaborators
Elvis Presley's 1st appearance on national TV on the Dorsey Brothers's "Stage Show"
"Tonight! America After Dark" premieres, with Jack Lescoulie & Al (Jazzbo) Collins on NBC (between Steve Allen &Jack Paar)
Dodger catcherRoy Campanellais paralyzed in an automobile wreck
"The Goon Show"'s final episode on BBC radio
New version of "La fille mal gardée" (1789) choreographed byFrederick Ashtondebuts with the Royal Ballet with Nadia Nerina debuts at Covent Garden, London
1969 NFL Draft:O.J. Simpsonfrom USC first pick by Buffalo Bills
Sam Thompson,Jim Bottomley, & Jocko Conlan elected to the Baseball Hall of Fame
New York drug lordFrank Lucasis sentenced to 40 years in prison and a $200,000 fine after being convicted on four drug charges
Arthur Kopit's play "Wings" premieres in NYC
PopeJohn Paul IIaddresses the Third General Conference of Latin American Episcopate in Puebla, Mexico
William J. Caseybecomes 13th director of CIA (until 1987)
Edmonton centerWayne Gretzky's NHL record point scoring streak ends at 51 games when Oilers beaten 4-2 by LA Kings; Gretzky totals 61-92-153 during the period
Charity single "We Are the World" recorded by supergroup USA for Africa (Michael Jackson, Lionel Richie,Quincy Jones,Bruce Springsteen,Stevie Wonder, and other pop stars)
US Foreign ministerGeorge P. Shultzmeets African National Congress leaderOliver Tambo
New York Rangers'Marcel Dionnescores a 2nd period power play goal in 5-2 win in Philadelphia; becomes 1st first player in NHL history to get 20 goals in each of his first 17 seasons
Super Bowl XXIV, Louisiana Superdome, New Orleans, LA: San Francisco 49ers beat Denver Broncos, 55-10; MVP:Joe Montana, SF, QB
St. Louis right wingBrett Hullbecomes 2nd player (after Wayne Gretzky) in NHL history to record back-to-back seasons of 50 goals in 50 games; #50 comes in 3-3 Blues' tie in Los Angeles
Michelangelo's drawing "Christ and the Woman of Samaria" sells for $7.4 million - a record prince for the artist
Super Bowl XXXV, Raymond James Stadium, Tampa, FL: Baltimore Ravens beat New York Giants, 34-7; MVP:Ray Lewis, Baltimore, LB
In his State of the Union address US PresidentGeorge W. Bushannounces President’s Emergency Plan for AIDS Relief (PEPFAR) - plan will go on to save 25 million lives worldwide (as of Jan 2023)

Five murderers of Bangladeshi PresidentSheikh Mujibur Rahmanhanged: Lieutenant Colonel Syed Faruq Rahman, Lieutenant Colonel Sultan Shahriar Rashid Khan, Major AKM Mohiuddin Ahmed, Major Bazlul Huda and Lieutenant Colonel Mohiuddin Ahmed
Hundreds of thousands of protesters fill the Egyptian's streets against theHosni Mubarak's regime in demonstrations referred to as the "Friday of Anger"
Australian Open Women's Tennis:Serena Williamsdefeats older sisterVenus Williams6–4, 6–4 for her 7th Australian title and record 23rd Grand Slam event singles victory
US PresidentDonald Trumpand Australian PMMalcolm Turnbullhave a contentious phone call over deal for US to take 1,250 refugees
60th Grammy Awards:Bruno Marswins Best Song "That's What I Like," and Best Album "24K Magic"
US PresidentDonald Trumpreleases his Middle East peace plan alongside Israeli PMBenjamin Netanyahuat the White House
Bernie Sandersmittens worn to the inauguration raise $1.8 million for Vermont charities after images go viral
Space Shuttle Challenger explodes 73 seconds after liftoff from Cape Canaveral, killing all seven crew members, including Christa McAuliffe, who was to be the first teacher in space
Li Shimin, Emperor Taizong of Tang, Emperor of China (626-49), born at Wugong (d. 649)
Joan II, Queen of Navarre (1328-49), born in Charenton-le-Pont, France (d. 1349)
Ludolph van Ceulen, German-Dutch mathematician who calculated π to 35 places, born in Hildesheim, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1610)
Cornelis Haga, Dutch lawyer and ambassador to Constantinople (1611-39), born in Schiedam, Netherlands (d. 1654)
John Barclay, Scottish satirist writer and poet (Euphormio's Satyricon), born in Pont-à-Mousson, Lorraine, France (d. 1621)
Clement IX [Giulio Rospigliosi], Italian Pope (238th: 1667-69), born in Pistoia, Italy (d. 1669)
Giovanni Alfonso Borelli, mathematician and astronomer, born in Naples, Kingdom of Naples, Spanish Empire (now Italy) (d. 1679)
Johannes Hevelius, Polish astronomer who pioneered lunar topography (Prodromus Astronomiae - catalogue of over 1500 stars), born in Danzig (d. 1687)
Adrien Auzout, French astronomer who improved measuring instruments (moon crater "Auzout"), born in Rouen, France (d. 1691)[1]
Alfonso Marsh, English composer and lutenist, baptized in London, England (d. 1681)
Gottfried Vopelius, German hymn composer, born in Herwigsdorf, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1715)
Jan Maurits Quinkhard, Dutch portrait painter, born in Rees, Netherlands (d. 1772)
Johann Balthasar König, German Baroque composer, mainly of hymn music, born in Cannstatt, Duchy of Württemberg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1758)
Gregor Werner, Austrian Baroque composer and kapellmeister, born in Ybbs an der Donau, Austria (d. 1766)
Charles Marie de La Condamine, French mathematician and first scientific explorer of the Amazon River, born in Paris, France (d. 1774)[1]
John Baskerville, English printer and type designer (printer to the University of Cambridge), baptized in Wolverley, Worcestershire, England (d. 1775)
Tokugawa Ieshige, 9th Japanese shōgun of the Tokugawa shogunate (1745-60), born in Minato, Japan (d. 1761)
Jean-Baptiste Pigalle, French sculptor (Child with Cage, Mercury Fastening his Sandals), born in Paris (d. 1785)
Mustafa III, Ottoman Sultan (1757-74), born in Edirne Palace, Edirne, Ottoman Empire (d. 1774)
Johann Ernst Bach, German kapellmeister and composer, born in Eisenach, Duchy of Saxe-Eisenach, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1777)
Hans Adolf Friedrich von Eschstruth, German composer and lawyer, born in Homberg, Landgraviate of Hesse-Darmstadt. Holy Roman Empire (d. 1792)
Antonio Bartolomeo Bruni, Italian composer and conductor, born in Cuneo, Kingdom of Sardinia (d. 1821)
Frederick VI, King of Denmark (1808-39) and Norway (1808-1814), born in Christiansborg Palace, Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1839)
Giovanni Battista Velluti, Italian castrato singer 'the last great castrato', born in Pausula, Papal States (d. 1861)
Ferdinand Hérold, French pianist, violinist, and composer of over twenty operas, born in Paris (d. 1833)
Andrew Jackson Hamilton, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Huntsville, Alabama (d. 1875)
George S. Boutwell, American politician, lawyer, and 28th Secretary of the Treasury (1869-73), born in Brookline, Massachusetts (d. 1905)
Michel Abeloos, Flemish sculptor, born in Leuven (d. 1881)
Thomas Carmichael Hindman, Jr., American attorney, and Confederate Army Major General, born in Knoxville Tennessee (d. 1868)
Henry Brevard Davidson, American Brigadier General in the Confederate Army (Civil War), born in Shelbyville, Tennessee (d. 1899)
Franz Wüllner, German composer and conductor, born in Münster, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1902)
Charles George Gordon, British general (put down Taiping rebellion in China) and administrator (Governor-General of Sudan), born in London, England (d. 1885)
Viktor Ernst Nessler, Alsatian composer, born in Baldenheim, Alsace (d. 1890)
Vasily Osipovich Klyuchevsky, Russian imperial historian, born in Penza Governorate, Russia (d. 1911)
George Wright, American Baseball HOF shortstop (NA pennant 1872-75 Boston Red Stockings; NL pennant 1877-78 Boston Red Caps, 1879 Providence Grays), born in Yonkers, New York (d. 1937)
Jose Martí y Perez, Cuban poet, essayist and politician (fought for independence from Spain), born in Havana, Cuba (d. 1895)
William Seward Burroughs, American inventor of the first workable adding machine and the electric alarm clock, born in Rochester, New York (d. 1898)
Eugène Dubois, Dutch paleontologist who discovered human ancestor Pithecanthropus erectus "Java Man", born in Eisden, Netherlands (d. 1940)[1]
Ernest William Christmas, Australian painter (Rowing away from the Storm), born in Yankalilla, Australia (d. 1918)[1][2]
Herbert Akroyd Stuart, English engineer and inventor of the heavy oil engine, born in Halifax, England (d. 1927)
Kaarlo Juho Ståhlberg, Finnish jurist and first President of Finland (1914-17, 1919-25), born in Suomussalmi, Finland (d. 1952)
Lala Lajpat Rai, Indian author, politician, and freedom fighter, born in Dhudike, British India  (d. 1928)
Frederic Lamond, Scottish composer, pupil of Liszt, born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 1948)
Julian Aguirre, Argentine composer, born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 1924)
Monty Noble, Australian cricket all-rounder (42 Tests, 1 x 100, HS 133, 121 wickets, BB 7/17; NSW), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1940)
Alex Smith, Scottish-American golfer (US Open 1906, 10), born in Carnoustie, Scotland (d. 1930)
Ernst Rabel, Austrian-American lawyer, human rights activist, and author (The Conflict of Laws: A Comparative Study), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1955)
Julian Antonio Carillo Trujillo, Mexican composer, musician and conductor (developed theory of microtonal music "The Thirteenth Sound"), born in Ahualulco, Mexico (d. 1965)
Walter Kollo, German composer of operettas, Singspiele, and popular songs (Der Juxbaron), born in Neidenburg, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1940)
Herbert Strudwick, English cricket wicketkeeper (28 Tests, 73 dismissals; Surrey CCC), born in Mitcham, England (d. 1970)
Mary Boland, American comedienne and actress (Ruggles of Red Gap), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1965)
Auguste Piccard, Swiss scientist, explorer and balloonist (1st flight into stratosphere, by balloon), born in Basel, Switzerland (d. 1962)
Jean Felix Piccard, Swiss scientist, explorer and balloonist (1st flight into stratosphere, by balloon), born in Basel, Switzerland (d. 1963)
Lucien d'Azambuja, French astronomer (studied the solar chromosphere), born in Paris, France (d. 1970)
Hidetsugu Yagi, Japanese electrical engineer (Yagi-Uda antenna), born in Osaka, Empire of Japan (d. 1976)
Marthe Bibesco, Romanian-French writer, socialite, and political hostess, born in Bucharest, Kingdom of Romania (d. 1973)
Artur Rubinstein, Polish-American concert pianist, born in Łódź, Congress Poland (d. 1982)
Lily Strickland, American composer of art songs, piano, and silent film music, and ethnomusicologist (The Music Courier), born in Anderson, South Carolina (d. 1958)
Robert Stroud, American murderer, convict, and ornithologist dubbed "Birdman of Alcatraz", born in Seattle Washington (d. 1963)
Bill Doak, American baseball pitcher (NL ERA leader 1914, 21; St. Louis Cardinals), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1954)
Camille Melloy [Paepe], Belgian poet (Parfum des Buis), born in Melle (d. 1941)
Karel Boleslav Jirak, Czech composer and pedagogue, born in Prague, Kingdom of Bohemia, Austro-Hungarian Empire (d. 1972)
Elliot Griffis, American composer (The Port of Pleasure), and educator, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1967)
Valentin Kataev, novelist/playwright (Embezzled), born in Odessa Ukraine (d. 1986)
Vittorio Rieti, Jewish Italian-American composer, born in Alexandria, Egypt (d. 1994)
Wies Moens, Flemish writer and historian, born in Sint-Gillis-bij-Dendermonde, Belgium (d. 1982)
Elias Simojoki, Finnish clergyman and far right politician, born in Rautio, Grand Duchy of Finland (d. 1940)
Zilner Randolph, American jazz trumpeter, arranger, and music educator, born in Dermott, Arkansas (d. 1994)
A. A. M. Stols, Dutch publisher (History of Ecuador), born in Maastricht, Netherlands (d. 1973)
Alice Neel, American painter (Mother and Child, Evening at Riverside Park), born in Merion Square, Pennsylvania (d. 1984)[1][2]
Hermann Kesten, German writer (Allert de Lange), born in Pidvolochysk, Austro-Hungarian Empire (d. 1996)
Michael Head, British organist, pianist, and composer, born in Eastbourne, England (d. 1976)
Kathleen Lonsdale, Irish-born British pacifist and crystallographer (one of 1st two women elected to Royal Society), born in Newbridge, Ireland (d. 1971)
Lotte Stam-Beese, German-Dutch architect and urban planner who helped reconstruct Rotterdam after the war, born in Reisicht, Germany (d. 1989)
Canuplin, Filipino magician and bodabil entertainer, born in Tondo, Philippine Islands (d. 1979)
Luther Simjian, Armenian-American Inventor (teleprompter and first self-posing portrait camera), born in Aintab, Ottoman Empire (d. 1997)
Harry van Kruiningen, Dutch painter and graphic artist, born in Kruiningen, Netherlands (d. 1996)
Henry Foley, New Zealand cricket batsman (1 Test; NZ's first Test match; Wellington CA), born in Wellington (d. 1948)
Constantin Regamey, Ukranian pianist, composer and music critic, born in Kiev, Russian Empire (d. 1987)
Paul Misraki [Misrachi], French jazz pianist, songwriter, and film score composer (Jean Renoir), born in Constantinople, Ottoman Empire (d. 1998)
Lionel Crabb "Buster", British Royal Navy diver (WW II-George Medal), born in London, England (d. 1956)
Arnold Moss, American actor (Loves of Carmen, Kim, Quebec, Viva Zapata), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1989)
John Banner, Austrian-born American actor (Hogan's Heroes, Lone Ranger, 36 Hours), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1973)
Johan van Hulst, Dutch teacher who helped save 600 children from the Nazis, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 2018)
Jan Masséus, Dutch composer (7 Minutes of Organized Sound), born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1999)
Sir Oliver Chesterton, British businessman (Chestertons Estate Agency), birthplace unknown (d. 2007)
Nelson Olmsted [or Olmstead], American voice actor (Sleep No More), born in Minneapolis, Minnesota (d. 1992)
Peter Crossley-Holland, British composer, ethno-musicologist, and educator (UCLA, 1969-83), born in London, England (d. 2001)
Harry Corbett, English puppeteer (Sooty), TV presenter and magician, born in Bradford, England (d. 1989)
Suzanne Flon, French actress (One Deadly Summer, Moulin Rouge), born in Le Kremlin-Bicêtre, Seine (d. 2005)
Wilson Ferreira Aldunate, Uruguayan politician and human rights activist who ran for President, born in Nico Pérez, Uruguay (d. 1988)
Alfred Marks, British actor and comedian (Desert Mice, Scream & Scream Again), born in Holborn, London (d. 1996)
Robert W. Holley, American biochemist who worked with RNA (Nobel Prize 1968), born in Los Gatos, California (d. 1991)
Henry Harris, British cell biologist (identified genes capable of suppressing cancers), born in Russia (d. 2014)[1]
Anthony Garner, British political activist (Director of Conservative Central Office, 1976-88; Director of Campaigning, 1992-98), born in Liverpool, England (d. 2015)
Don Fraser, American boxing promoter (LA venues; Sugar Ray Robinson, Ruben Olivares, George Foreman; known for colourful wardrobe, offbeat PR gimmicks), born in Blythe, California (d. 2019)
John D Hughes, British economist, educator, and administrator (Principal of Ruskin College-Oxford, 1979-89), born in London (d. 2013)
Ronnie Scott [Schatt], British jazz saxophonist and club-owner, born in Aldgate, East London, England (d. 1996)
Roy Caton, American trumpet player and session musician, born in Frackville, Pennsylvania (d. 2010)
Martin Sinnatt, British army Major-General (Korean War), and administrator (Secretary and CEO of the Kennel Club, 1984-93), born in Sleaford, Lincolnshire, England (d. 2022)

Pete Runnels, American baseball infielder (5 x MLB All Star; AL batting champion 1960, 62 Boston Red Sox; Washington Senators, Houston Colt .45s) and manager (Boston RS 1966), born in Lufkin, Texas (d. 1991)
Slade Gorton, American politician (Sen-R-WA, 1981-87, 1989-2001), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2020)
